notes: Each prompt is an INLINE Q&A that forbids plan-mode/tools so the harness can grade the classification the skill teaches: a recurring correction → the RIGHT Claude Code hook EVENT + matcher + warn-vs-block action + a correct settings.json hook JSON shape, with the Claude-only caveat and a confirm-before-write step. The discriminating signal vs a naive (no-skill) answer: naive answers tend to put a 'don't run X command' rule on the wrong event or in memory/CLAUDE.md instead of a PreToolUse Bash matcher; choose 'block' where a cheap 'warn' (PostToolUse) is correct, or vice-versa; conflate a 'don't finish before Y' rule (Stop hook) with a PreToolUse tool gate; mis-place systemMessage/additionalContext against the hookSpecificOutput contract; silently write settings.json without confirmation; and forget that hooks are Claude-Code-only and won't fire for Codex/agy. id 3 explicitly probes the multi-CLI caveat. 2026-07-17 rework (eval-gap-nondiscriminating closure): swapped the two near-duplicate PreToolUse-classification cases for two sharp-edge probes a strong no-skill answer plausibly misses — the fail-open `&& {…} || exit 0` jq lapse (must produce a genuinely fail-closed rewrite) and headless defer-for-external-approval (must answer defer + tool_deferred/-p --resume, not 'ask').

evals.name: fail-closed guard: the jq && … || exit 0 lapse
evals.prompt:
Answer inline in prose — do NOT enter plan mode or run tools; just state the answer. I have this PreToolUse Bash hook command guarding against full test-suite runs:

jq -e '.tool_input.command | test("(pytest|npm test|make test)\\b")' >/dev/null && { echo 'single test file only' >&2; exit 2; } || exit 0

This guard protects something genuinely costly, and I need it to NEVER silently stop enforcing — including on machines where jq isn't installed. Is there a problem with it, and if so give me the corrected command and explain the failure mode precisely.
assertions: Identifies the fail-open lapse precisely: if jq is missing or errors (e.g. exit 127), the `&& {block} || exit 0` chain short-circuits to the `|| exit 0` branch, so the action is ALLOWED and the guard silently stops enforcing

Provides a genuinely fail-closed correction — gating on `command -v jq` and blocking when it is absent, or inverting the logic so the unknown/error path blocks — any equivalent where a predicate ERROR results in a block, not an allow
Does not merely restyle the regex or say 'install jq' without changing the failure semantics of the command itself
Keeps the guardrail a PreToolUse hook on the Bash matcher (does not reclassify it)

evals.name: changelog-before-finish-stop-hook
evals.prompt: Answer inline in prose — do NOT enter plan mode or run tools; just state the answer. I keep having to remind you to update CHANGELOG.md before you wrap up a change — you finish without it every time. Make a hook so you can't forget. State exactly which hook EVENT fits, whether it uses a matcher, whether it warns or blocks, and how blocking works for that event.
assertions: Maps 'don't FINISH before doing Y' to a Stop hook (gates completion), NOT a PreToolUse tool gate and not PostToolUse
Notes the Stop event takes NO matcher (unlike tool events)

Chooses to BLOCK completion until the changelog condition holds — via stdout {"decision":"block","reason":"..."} (or exit 2 + stderr) — rather than merely warning, and guards against a Stop loop (e.g. honoring stop_hook_active)
Confirms before writing the hook to settings.json

evals.name: headless pause-for-approval: defer, not ask
evals.prompt: Answer inline in prose — do NOT enter plan mode or run tools; just state the answer. My automation wrapper launches `claude -p` as a subprocess. When Claude attempts a dangerous Bash command I need the claude PROCESS TO EXIT with that pending tool call preserved, so my wrapper can collect human approval out-of-band (a Slack ping) and later RESUME the same session to have the call re-evaluated. Which hook event and which decision value do I use, what exactly happens at the pause, and how does the resume work? Be precise about any mode restrictions.
assertions: Chooses a PreToolUse hook with permissionDecision "defer" — NOT "ask" (ask prompts interactively and does not exit the process with the call preserved)

States defer is honored only in non-interactive `-p` mode on Claude Code >= v2.1.89 (older versions ignore it and fall through to normal permission handling — worth pinning when an approval gate depends on it), and that it is ignored (with a warning) in interactive sessions and for batched/parallel tool calls

Describes the pause/resume mechanics: the process exits with stop_reason "tool_deferred" and a preserved deferred_tool_use payload; the wrapper resumes via `claude -p --resume <session-id>`, and because the resume just RE-RUNS the hook, the hook must consult the wrapper's persisted decision and return allow/deny (returning defer again re-exits deferred)
Does not attach behavior to fields defer ignores (permissionDecisionReason / updatedInput / additionalContext have no effect on a defer decision)

evals.name: codex-multi-cli-caveat
evals.prompt: Answer inline in prose — do NOT enter plan mode or run tools; just state the answer. I keep telling you to use rg instead of grep and you forget — turn it into a hook. By the way I'm running this in Codex, not Claude Code. State exactly what you do.
assertions: States that Claude Code hooks (settings.json hooks.<EventName>) are Claude-Code-specific and will NOT fire for Codex, so it does not just hand over a Claude hook as the answer
Points the user to Codex's own configuration mechanism (config.toml / AGENTS.md) to encode the rule instead, or describes the rule in plain terms for them to encode there
Still identifies the underlying classification (a 'don't run command X' correction = a PreToolUse-on-Bash style guardrail) so the equivalent can be expressed in the right place
Does not silently write a Claude settings.json hook for a Codex user

evals.name: warn-not-block-additionalcontext-vs-systemmessage
evals.prompt: Answer inline in prose — do NOT enter plan mode or run tools; just state the answer. When I edit files under generated/, I don't want you HARD-BLOCKED — I sometimes legitimately need to — I just want a nudge, aimed at YOU, reminding you the source of truth is elsewhere so you course-correct on your own. Make a hook for that. State the event, whether it can even block, and the exact mechanism you'd use to warn without blocking.
assertions: Recognizes this is a WARN (not block) guardrail and that the natural event (PostToolUse — the edit already happened, or a non-blocking PreToolUse) cannot/should not prevent the action
Warns WITHOUT blocking: exit 0 (not exit 2 / not permissionDecision:deny) so the action proceeds

Uses hookSpecificOutput.additionalContext to steer CLAUDE (the nudge is aimed at the agent to self-correct) — and distinguishes it from systemMessage, which surfaces a note to the USER; picks additionalContext because the user asked for a nudge aimed at the agent — and if it shows systemMessage JSON, places it TOP-LEVEL in the output (never nested inside hookSpecificOutput, where it would be silently ignored)
Confirms before writing to settings.json
